Preface
School education is the foundation for preparing the citizen who are loyal to the
nation and nationality, committed to the norms and values of federal democratic
republic, self-reliant and respecting the social and cultural diversity.

It is also
remarkable for developing a good moral character with the practical know-how
of the use of ICT along with the application of scientific concept and positive
thinking.

It is also expected to prepare the citizens who are moral and ethical,
disciplined, social and human value sensitive with the consciousness about
the environmental conversation and sustainable development.

Moreover, it
should be helpful for developing the skills for solving the real life problems.

This textbook 'Mathematics, Grade 5' is fully aligned with the intent carried
out by the National Curriculum Framework for School Education, 2076 and
is developed fully in accordance with the new Basic Level Curriculum, 2078.

(a) As shown in the picture on the right, when the

The angles having the measures exactly 90°are called the right angles.

The angles having the measures less than 90° and greater than
0° are called the acute angles.

The angles having the measures greater than 90° and less than
180° are called obtuse angles.

In the above angles, ∠ABC is right angle, ∠XYZ is acute angle
and ∠LMN is obtuse angle.

All the angles of the above square grid are equal.

The angles
∠XYA, ∠XYB, ∠POB, ∠POA, ∠RSB and ∠RSA formed by
the lines XY, PO and RS on the line AB have equal measure,
i.e.

All angles formed by vertical lines with horizontal
lines are 90° and so, they are all equal.

Two straight lines that
make 90° at the point of intersection to each other are called
perpendicular lines.

Two lines that make an angle of 90° to each other are
called the perpendicular lines.

In the above figure, XY,
PO and RS are perpendicular with AB.

It can be written
as XY⊥ AB, PO ⊥ AB and RS ⊥AB.

In a square grid, all the vertical and horizontal lines are
perpendicular to each other.

(B) Parallel Lines
Activity 10

Draw straight lines AB and CD on the square grid paper

A

Now, discuss based on the questions given below:
(a) How many squares are there from A to C?
(b) How many squares are there from B to D?
(c) Are there the same number of squares?
(d) What kind of lines are called the above lines?
There are four squares from A to C.

Similarly, there are four squares
from B to D.
The distance from A to C and from B to D are equal.

In
such condition, the lines AB and CD are parallel to each other.

This
is denoted by AB // CD.

If the distance between the two straight lines on the plane surface
is always the same, then those lines are called parallel lines.
Horizontal lines in the square grid are parallel to each other.

Similarly, vertical lines in the square grid are parallel to each
other.

the uncrossed numbers are the prime numbers.

define the prime and composite numbers.

The numbers which are exactly divisible by other numbers except by
1 and itself are called the composite numbers.

The numbers which
are exactly divisible by only and itself are called the prime numbers.

The given method of finding the prime number was first introduced
by Greek mathematician Eratosthenes.

Thus, the above method is
called Sieve of Eratosthenes.

Equality facts
(i) Equality fact of addition: If the same quatity is added on
both sides of an equation, two sides are still equal.

(ii) Equality fact of subtraction: If the same quantity is subtract­
ed on both sides of an equation, two sides are still equal.

(iii) Equality fact of multiplication: If both sides of an equation
are multiplied by same quantity, two sides are still equal.

(iv) Equality fact of division: If both sides of an equation are
divided by same quantity, two sides are still equal.
